seo.title: Government Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in government. Covers FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53.

content.intro: Deploying AI code generation agents in government environments carries extraordinary risk — a single misconfiguration can expose classified systems, violate civil liberties, or trigger congressional oversight. Federal agencies must satisfy overlapping mandates from FedRAMP, FISMA, NIST 800-53, and the Executive Order on AI before any LLM-assisted code generation tool touches production infrastructure. This checklist provides concrete, actionable controls tailored to the unique intersection of government compliance requirements and agentic code generation architectures.

sections.heading: Data Classification & Boundary Controls
sections.description: Ensure the code generation agent never processes, transmits, or stores data above its authorized classification level, and that all inputs and outputs are inspected at the boundary.

items.label: Classify all input prompts before LLM submission
items.description: Implement an automated classification scanner (e.g., using regex patterns and ML classifiers) that inspects every prompt for CUI, PII, or classified markers before forwarding to the LLM. Reject and log any prompt containing classification markings above UNCLASSIFIED.

items.label: Prohibit external LLM calls for classified workloads
items.description: Enforce a network-level policy (firewall ACL + application-layer check) that routes any code generation request tagged CUI or above exclusively to an on-premise or FedRAMP High-authorized model deployment. Document this routing logic in your System Security Plan (SSP).

items.label: Scan generated code output for credential and secret leakage
items.description: Run every LLM-generated code artifact through a secrets-detection tool (e.g., truffleHog, detect-secrets) configured with government-specific patterns including CAC/PIV token formats, AWS GovCloud key prefixes, and DoD certificate thumbprints before the code enters the sandbox.

items.label: Enforce data residency for all agent memory and context windows
items.description: Configure the agent's context storage (vector DB, session cache) to reside exclusively within FedRAMP-authorized infrastructure. Verify storage region tags in your cloud console and include residency attestation in quarterly FISMA reporting.

items.label: Implement input/output logging with tamper-evident audit trails
items.description: Log every prompt, generated code snippet, execution result, and refinement iteration to a SIEM (e.g., Splunk GovCloud) with WORM storage. Logs must include user identity (PIV-authenticated), timestamp, classification level, and session ID to support incident reconstruction.

items.label: Apply content filtering to prevent reverse-engineering of system prompts
items.description: Deploy a prompt injection detection layer that blocks attempts to extract system instructions, internal API keys embedded in context, or agency network topology details. Test this control quarterly using OWASP LLM Top 10 injection scenarios.

items.label: Define and enforce maximum context window retention policies
items.description: Set context window expiration to no longer than the session duration and purge all context data upon session termination. Document the purge mechanism in the SSP and verify it with automated post-session audits.

sections.heading: Sandbox Execution & Code Safety Validation
sections.description: All LLM-generated code must execute in an isolated, monitored sandbox with strict resource limits before any human review or promotion to higher environments.

items.label: Deploy a gVisor or Firecracker microVM sandbox for all generated code execution
items.description: Run every code artifact in a kernel-isolated sandbox (gVisor runsc or Firecracker microVM) with no network egress, read-only filesystem mounts, and CPU/memory caps. Validate sandbox integrity with a startup attestation check before each execution session.

items.label: Perform static analysis with SAST tools before sandbox execution
items.description: Run generated code through at least two SAST tools (e.g., Semgrep with government rulesets + Bandit for Python or SonarQube for Java) and block execution if any HIGH or CRITICAL findings are detected. Map SAST rules to NIST 800-53 SI-10 (Information Input Validation) controls.

items.label: Validate syntax and compilation before execution
items.description: Execute a dry-run compile or syntax check (e.g., pylint --errors-only, tsc --noEmit, gcc -fsyntax-only) as the first pipeline stage. Reject and return structured error feedback to the LLM agent for refinement rather than allowing malformed code to reach the sandbox.

items.label: Scan all generated dependency references against approved software lists
items.description: Cross-reference every import, require, or package reference in generated code against your agency's Approved Software List (ASL) and the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog. Block execution and flag for human review if any unapproved or vulnerable dependency is detected.

items.label: Enforce syscall allowlists via seccomp profiles in the execution sandbox
items.description: Apply a seccomp-bpf profile that whitelists only the syscalls required for the target language runtime. Deny and alert on any syscall outside the allowlist, particularly those associated with privilege escalation (e.g., ptrace, setuid, mount).

items.label: Implement execution time and resource quotas with automatic termination
items.description: Set hard limits of 30 seconds execution time, 512MB RAM, and 100MB disk write per sandbox run. Automatically terminate and quarantine any execution that exceeds these limits, and log the event as a potential denial-of-service or infinite loop condition.

items.label: Capture and analyze sandbox execution traces for anomalous behavior
items.description: Use eBPF-based tracing (e.g., Falco or Tetragon) to record all file access, network attempts, and process spawns during sandbox execution. Alert on any network connection attempt, unexpected file write outside /tmp, or child process creation.

items.label: Require human-in-the-loop approval before promoting code to staging or production
items.description: Enforce a mandatory human review gate where a credentialed developer (PIV-authenticated) must explicitly approve generated code artifacts before they exit the sandbox pipeline. Document this approval in the audit log with reviewer identity and timestamp.

sections.heading: Algorithmic Accountability & Explainability
sections.description: Federal regulations and the Executive Order on AI require that AI-assisted decisions affecting government operations or citizens be explainable, auditable, and subject to appeal or override.

items.label: Conduct and document an Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) before deployment
items.description: Complete a formal AIA following OMB guidance, documenting the model's intended use, potential for bias in code generation (e.g., insecure patterns for certain languages), failure modes, and mitigation controls. Store the AIA in your agency's risk management framework documentation and review annually.

items.label: Generate human-readable rationale for every code suggestion
items.description: Configure the agent to produce a structured explanation alongside each code artifact, including: which requirement it addresses, which security controls it implements, and which alternatives were considered. This rationale must be stored with the code artifact in the audit log.

items.label: Implement a formal override and appeal mechanism for rejected code submissions
items.description: Build a documented escalation path where developers can challenge automated rejections (e.g., false-positive SAST findings) through a ticketed review process. Track override rates and reasons as a key metric in your AI governance dashboard.

items.label: Maintain a model card and version registry for all LLMs used in the pipeline
items.description: Document each LLM's training data provenance, known limitations, benchmark performance on government coding tasks, and authorization status in a centralized model registry. Update the registry whenever the model version changes and notify the ISSO.

items.label: Track and report AI-generated code percentage in all software deliverables
items.description: Instrument the pipeline to tag and track what percentage of each software deliverable was AI-generated versus human-written. Include this metric in program management reports and contract deliverables to maintain transparency with oversight bodies.

items.label: Conduct quarterly bias and quality audits of generated code outputs
items.description: Sample at least 5% of generated code artifacts per quarter for manual review by senior engineers. Evaluate for security anti-patterns, language-specific insecure defaults, and consistency with agency coding standards. Report findings to the AI governance board.

items.label: Establish a feedback loop to retrain or fine-tune models on agency-specific secure coding standards
items.description: Collect human reviewer corrections and approved code examples into a curated dataset. Submit this dataset to the model provider or internal ML team for fine-tuning on a quarterly basis, with each fine-tuning event documented in the model registry.

sections.heading: Access Control, Identity, & Incident Response
sections.description: Restrict access to the code generation agent to authorized personnel only, enforce least-privilege principles, and maintain a tested incident response plan specific to AI-generated code incidents.

items.label: Require PIV/CAC authentication for all access to the code generation agent
items.description: Integrate the agent's frontend and API with your agency's PIV/CAC authentication infrastructure (e.g., via SAML 2.0 or OIDC with certificate-based auth). Reject all username/password or API-key-only authentication attempts and log them as security events.

items.label: Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) with least-privilege for agent capabilities
items.description: Define distinct roles (e.g., Developer, Reviewer, Admin) with granular permissions controlling which agent features each role can access (e.g., only Admins can modify sandbox configuration, only Reviewers can approve code for promotion). Map roles to NIST 800-53 AC-6 controls.

items.label: Implement session recording and replay capability for all agent interactions
items.description: Record full session transcripts (prompts, responses, code artifacts, execution results) in a format that can be replayed for forensic investigation. Encrypt recordings at rest with FIPS 140-2 validated encryption and retain for a minimum of 3 years per NARA guidelines.

items.label: Define and test an AI-specific incident response playbook
items.description: Create a dedicated IR playbook covering scenarios including: LLM prompt injection attack, generation of malicious code, credential leakage in output, and sandbox escape. Conduct a tabletop exercise with the SOC team semi-annually and update the playbook based on findings.

items.label: Establish automated alerting for anomalous agent usage patterns
items.description: Configure SIEM rules to alert on: >50 code generation requests per user per hour, requests submitted outside business hours from non-authorized locations, repeated SAST failures from the same user, and any sandbox escape attempt. Route alerts to the SOC with P1 priority.

items.label: Perform annual penetration testing targeting the code generation pipeline
items.description: Engage a FedRAMP-authorized penetration testing firm to conduct adversarial testing of the full pipeline annually, specifically targeting prompt injection, sandbox escape, dependency confusion attacks, and privilege escalation via generated code. Remediate all HIGH/CRITICAL findings within 30 days.

items.label: Maintain a software bill of materials (SBOM) for all AI-generated code artifacts
items.description: Automatically generate a CycloneDX or SPDX SBOM for every code artifact that exits the pipeline, capturing all dependencies, their versions, and known CVEs at time of generation. Store SBOMs alongside artifacts and feed them into your vulnerability management program.

notes.note: Any LLM or supporting infrastructure (vector databases, sandboxes, logging systems) used in the code generation pipeline must operate within a FedRAMP Authorized boundary at the appropriate impact level (Moderate or High). Your agency's ATO must explicitly cover the AI code generation system as a component, and the ISSO must be notified of any model version changes that could alter the system's risk posture under FISMA continuous monitoring requirements.

The code generation pipeline must implement controls from the SA (System and Services Acquisition) and SI (System and Information Integrity) families, specifically SA-11 (Developer Testing and Evaluation), SA-15 (Development Process Standards), SI-10 (Information Input Validation), and SI-7 (Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity). Map each checklist item to its corresponding 800-53 control in your SSP and validate coverage during annual assessments.

notes.regulation: Executive Order 14110 on Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy AI
notes.note: Federal agencies must comply with OMB Memorandum M-24-10, which requires completing an Algorithmic Impact Assessment for AI systems that affect government operations, designating a Chief AI Officer (CAIO) to oversee deployment, and reporting AI use cases to the AI.gov inventory. Code generation agents that produce artifacts used in citizen-facing systems or national security applications require the highest level of scrutiny and must be included in your agency's annual AI use case inventory submission.

If the AI code generation agent produces frontend code (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) or documentation that will be used in citizen-facing government applications, all generated artifacts must be validated against WCAG 2.1 AA standards before deployment. Integrate automated accessibility testing tools (e.g., axe-core, Pa11y) into the sandbox validation pipeline as a mandatory gate for any UI-related code generation tasks, and document accessibility validation results in the audit log.

cta.heading: Automate Your Government Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI code generation pipeline against FedRAMP, FISMA, and NIST 800-53 controls — catching compliance gaps before they become congressional investigations.
